question: My employer has not paid my holiday pay. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your holiday pay, you should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement. Holiday pay is your right for the time you take off work as holiday, and your employer must pay you for this. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, try talking to your employer to explain the issue and show any evidence you have, such as your contract or payslips.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid holiday pay. It is important to keep records of your communications and any evidence of your entitlement. You can also contact ACAS for advice on holiday pay issues.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue with evidence.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem is not resolved.  
Step 4: Consider legal action or contact ACAS for further advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How is holiday pay calculated if I work irregular hours?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on the average pay you have received for the hours you have worked. This means your employer should look at your pay over the previous 52 weeks (or fewer if you have worked less time) and calculate an average weekly pay from that. Only the hours you actually worked and were paid for are counted in this calculation. Your holiday pay should reflect your "normal" rate of pay, which includes things like commission and regular overtime but usually excludes bonuses.

For irregular-hours workers, all statutory holiday leave must be paid at this normal rate of pay. You can use the holiday entitlement calculator on GOV.UK to help work out how much holiday pay you should receive. If your leave year began on or before 31 March 2024, different rules may apply, but generally, holiday pay is based on the average pay for hours worked.

Step 1: Calculate your average hourly or weekly pay over the last 52 weeks (or the weeks you have worked if less than 52).  
Step 2: Multiply this average by the number of hours you would normally work in a week to get your average weekly pay.  
Step 3: Use this average weekly pay to calculate the pay for your statutory holiday entitlement (5.6 weeks per year).  

If you have questions or need help calculating your holiday pay, you can contact the Acas helpline.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: Can my employer include holiday pay in my hourly rate?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, an employer cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate if you are a regular worker. This practice is known as "rolled-up holiday pay" and is generally not allowed. However, for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers, employers can use rolled-up holiday pay, but only if the leave year began on or before 31 March 2024. After this date, the rules have changed, and rolled-up holiday pay is no longer permitted for these workers either.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay should be calculated based on the hours you have worked rather than being included in your hourly rate. If you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact ACAS for guidance.

Step 1: Check if you are a regular worker or if you have irregular hours or part-year work.  
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year started (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: If you believe your employer is including holiday pay in your hourly rate unlawfully, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for help.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: I have taken annual leave but did not get paid for it. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave. This means if you have taken holiday, your employer must pay you for that time. Holiday pay is part of your statutory rights, and you continue to build up holiday entitlement even during certain types of leave such as maternity or sick leave. If you believe you have not been paid for holiday you have taken, you should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement.

If your employer has not paid you for your holiday, you should raise the issue with them directly and ask for the payment. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to take further steps to enforce your rights, including seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your contract and any company holiday policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue and request payment.  
Step 3: If your employer does not pay you, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your options to recover the pay owed.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How much holiday am I legally entitled to in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, most workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which is known as statutory annual leave. For someone working a standard 5-day week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday annually. If you work part-time, your entitlement is proportional to the number of days or hours you work. For example, if you work 2 days a week, you would be entitled to 11.2 days of paid holiday (2 days × 5.6 weeks). This entitlement includes time accrued during periods such as maternity, paternity, adoption leave, or sick leave.

Employers may include bank holidays as part of your statutory leave, but they are not required to do so. Some employers offer enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement above the legal minimum, so you should check your employment contract for any additional holiday rights. If you are unsure about your exact entitlement, you can use the holiday entitlement calculator on the GOV.UK website or speak to your employer or HR department.

Step 1: Check your contract or written statement of employment particulars to see if you have any enhanced holiday entitlement.  
Step 2: Calculate your statutory holiday entitlement based on your working pattern (full-time or part-time).  
Step 3: Use the GOV.UK holiday entitlement calculator if you work irregular hours or part-year.  
Step 4: Contact your employer, HR, or the Acas helpline if you have questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: Does holiday continue to build up while I am off sick?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, holiday entitlement continues to build up while you are off work sick. Under UK employment law, workers accrue their statutory paid holiday during periods of sickness absence. This means that even if you are unable to work due to illness, you still accumulate your holiday entitlement as if you were working.

If you want to check exactly how much holiday you have accrued or how to use it, you can speak to your employer or HR department, or consult your employment contract. If you have further questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement during sickness, you can contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Confirm your holiday entitlement and accrual with your employer or HR.  
Step 2: Keep records of your sickness absence and holiday taken.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice if you believe your rights are not being met.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: What happens to my unused holiday when I leave my job?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave your job, you are entitled to be paid for any unused statutory holiday entitlement that you have accrued but not taken. This means if you have built up holiday during your employment that you haven't used, your employer should pay you for this untaken holiday as part of your final pay. The amount you receive should be based on your usual pay rate.

If you have any enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement above the statutory minimum, whether you get paid for unused holiday depends on your employment contract. Some contracts allow you to carry over holiday or be paid for it, while others may not. It is important to check your contract or speak to your employer about this.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for any terms about unused holiday pay.  
Step 2: Calculate how much statutory holiday you have accrued but not taken.  
Step 3: Discuss with your employer or HR how your unused holiday will be handled in your final pay.  
Step 4: If you believe you have not been paid correctly, you can seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Can my employer refuse my holiday request?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can refuse your holiday request, but they must have a good reason for doing so. Workers have a legal right to paid annual leave, but employers can refuse holiday requests if business needs require it. It is best to check your employment contract or company policy, as these may set out how holiday requests should be made and handled. If you disagree with your employer’s decision, you can try to resolve the dispute through discussion or by seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your contract or company policy on holiday requests and refusals.  
Step 2: Discuss your holiday request and the refusal with your employer to understand the reasons.  
Step 3: If you believe your rights are not being met, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Can my employer force me to take holiday?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers do have the right to require employees to take holiday at certain times, but they must give proper notice. Generally, an employer must give at least twice as much notice as the length of the holiday they want you to take. For example, if they want you to take one week off, they should give you at least two weeks' notice. This is to ensure you have enough time to plan and prepare.

Employers cannot force you to take holiday without notice or outside of agreed terms in your contract or workplace policies. If you are unsure about your holiday entitlement or how your employer can require you to take holiday, you should check your contract or speak to your HR department. If disputes arise, there are ways to resolve them, including contacting ACAS for guidance.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or staff handbook for any rules about taking holiday.  
Step 2: If your employer asks you to take holiday, ensure they give you the correct notice (twice the length of the holiday).  
Step 3: If you disagree or have concerns, discuss this with your employer or HR.  
Step 4: For further help, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Do part-time workers get holiday pay?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, part-time workers in the UK are entitled to holiday pay. By law, all workers, including part-time workers, have the right to 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year, which is known as statutory annual leave. For part-time workers, this entitlement is calculated proportionally based on the number of days or hours they work. For example, if a part-time worker works three days a week, they are entitled to 16.8 days of paid holiday annually (3 days × 5.6 weeks). Employers cannot treat part-time workers less favourably than full-time workers regarding holiday entitlement.

Holiday pay must be provided during this statutory leave, and workers continue to build up holiday entitlement even when on certain types of leave such as maternity or sick leave. If an employer offers more than the statutory minimum holiday, part-time workers should receive a proportionate amount of this enhanced holiday entitlement as well.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: Can my employer pay me instead of giving me holiday?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave, which is called statutory holiday entitlement. Employers must provide this leave rather than simply paying you in lieu of taking the holiday, except in certain circumstances such as when your employment ends. Generally, you should take your holiday as time off work to rest and recover. However, if your employment ends and you have unused holiday entitlement, your employer must pay you for that untaken holiday.

If you think your employer is not meeting your rights to holiday or holiday pay, you can raise the issue with them or seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies to understand your holiday entitlement and any rules about payment in lieu.  
Step 2: If your employer is not allowing you to take holiday or is paying you instead without your agreement, raise the issue informally with them.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on how to proceed.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Is rolled-up holiday pay legal in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Rolled-up holiday pay, where holiday pay is included within an employee's hourly rate rather than paid separately during holiday periods, is generally not allowed in the UK for most workers. However, there is an exception for irregular-hours and part-year workers, who can receive rolled-up holiday pay unless their leave year started on or before 31 March 2024. This means that for most workers, holiday pay must be paid separately and not "rolled up" into their hourly wage.

If you are unsure whether rolled-up holiday pay applies to your situation or if your employer is following the rules correctly, you can contact ACAS for guidance.

Step 1: Check if you are an irregular-hours or part-year worker.
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year started.
Step 3: Contact ACAS or consult your employment contract for clarification.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: My employer has not paid my wages on payday. What should I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to your employer or payroll department to find out why the payment is late. It might be a mistake they can quickly fix. You should not have to wait until your next payday to receive the money owed. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the problem continues and your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your claim. Note that there is a strict deadline of 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due to be paid to start tribunal proceedings.

Step 1: Speak to your employer or payroll department to ask why your wages are late.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer if possible.  
Step 3: If still unpaid, consider seeking advice and potentially making a claim to an employment tribunal before the deadline.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer deducted money from my wages without explaining why.

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer must have a valid reason and your agreement to deduct money from your wages. This could be because of an overpayment they want to recover, or deductions for things like damaged property or missing money from the till, but only if your contract says they can do this or you have agreed in writing. They must also follow specific rules if the deduction is for missing money or damaged stock, such as not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each pay day and giving you written details of the deduction each time.

If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your employment contract and any written agreements you have. If you still don’t think the deduction is lawful, you should talk to an adviser for help and advice.

Step 1: Check your contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t get a satisfactory answer, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I think I am being paid below minimum wage. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group and employment status on the GOV.UK website or through ACAS. Employers are legally required to pay at least the minimum wage for the hours you work. If you find that your pay is below the legal minimum, you should talk to your employer to raise the issue and ask them to correct your pay immediately.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of three months minus one day from when you should have been paid to start tribunal proceedings. You can also seek advice from a trade union representative if you are a member.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer about your concerns and provide any evidence you have.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action through an employment tribunal or small claims court if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What can I do if my employer pays me late every month?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why the payments are late and explain how it affects you. It’s important to keep a record of when you were supposed to be paid and when you actually received your wages. Your employer should pay you on the agreed pay day as per your contract. If the late payments continue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure.

If talking to your employer and raising a grievance does not resolve the issue, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim for unlawful deduction of wages through an employment tribunal. However, this should be a last resort, especially if you want to continue working for your employer. Keep in mind that the deadline to start an employment tribunal claim is usually 3 months minus a day from the date the payment was due.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to discuss the late payments.  
Step 2: Keep records of payment dates and amounts.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem continues.  
Step 4: Consider legal action if the issue remains unresolved, but seek advice before doing so.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer make deductions from my wages?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can make deductions from your wages only if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. Common reasons for deductions include recovering money they overpaid you, or costs for damaged property, missing money from the till, or uniforms, but these must be clearly stated in your contract or agreed to by you. For deductions related to missing money from the till or damaged stock, your employer must follow extra rules such as having a written agreement, not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each pay day, starting deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and providing written details of each deduction.

If you believe your employer has made deductions they are not entitled to, you should first check your employment contract and any written agreements. If you disagree with the deduction, you should talk to your employer to explain why and ask for the money you believe you are owed. If this does not resolve the issue, you can seek advice from an adviser or consider raising a grievance with your employer.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify and dispute any deductions you think are incorrect.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.  
Step 4: Consider raising a formal grievance if your employer has a procedure for this.  
Step 5: As a last resort, you may be able to take legal action for unlawful deductions from wages.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer has not paid me for overtime. Is that legal?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your employment contract and the terms agreed upon. There is no automatic legal right to overtime pay unless it is specified in your contract or workplace policies. However, your employer must pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked, including overtime. If your employer has not paid you for overtime and this means your total pay falls below the minimum wage, this would be unlawful.

If you believe you are owed overtime pay, you should first check your contract and any written agreements about overtime. Then, talk to your employer to clarify why you have not been paid. If this does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any overtime policies.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer or payroll department about the unpaid overtime.  
Step 3: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 4: Consider seeking advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.  
Step 5: If necessary, explore legal options such as an employment tribunal claim for unpaid wages.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What should be included on my payslip?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your payslip should clearly show your pay and any deductions made from it. This includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance contributions deducted, and any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments. It should also show your net pay (the amount you take home after deductions). Additionally, your payslip should include details like your pay period and your employer’s name.

Payslips help you check that you have been paid correctly and understand any deductions taken from your wages. If you think there is an error or an unlawful deduction, you should first talk to your employer to clarify the issue.

Step 1: Check your payslip each time you are paid to ensure all details are correct.  
Step 2: If you notice any discrepancies or deductions you do not understand, ask your employer or payroll department for an explanation.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you can seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a trade union representative.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Can my employer withhold my final pay when I leave?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave a job, your employer must pay you your final pay, which may be different from your usual pay because it can include things like holiday pay, redundancy pay, or deductions for training courses. Your final pay should be paid on your normal payday, even if you leave partway through a pay period. Employers should clearly explain how your final pay was calculated, and this should be shown on your payslip.

Your employer cannot withhold your final pay without a lawful reason. If you do not receive your final pay or payslip, you should first speak to your employer to clarify the situation. If this does not resolve the issue, you can seek further advice or take legal action to recover the money owed to you.

Step 1: Check your payslip and final pay calculation to understand what you should be paid.  
Step 2: Contact your employer to ask for an explanation if you have not received your final pay or if you disagree with the amount.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What can I do if my employer owes me commission?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer owes you commission, this is part of your wages and they should pay it on your agreed pay day. First, check your employment contract or any commission agreement to understand the terms about how and when commission is paid. If your employer hasn’t paid the commission you are owed, you should talk to them to find out why and show any evidence you have, such as sales records or commission statements.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim for unlawful deduction from wages at an employment tribunal. Remember, the deadline to start a tribunal claim is usually 3 months minus a day from when the commission should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead.

Step 1: Check your contract or commission agreement for payment terms.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and provide evidence of the commission owed.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action if the issue remains unresolved, keeping in mind the time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Is it legal for my employer to charge me for training?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer can charge you for training courses in some circumstances, but there are important rules. If the training is mandatory for your job, your employer cannot use it as a reason to pay you less than the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the hours you spend training. This means you should still receive at least the legal minimum hourly pay during mandatory training time. However, your employer may deduct money from your pay for training courses if this is clearly stated in your contract or agreed with you.

If you are unsure about deductions for training or feel your pay is being unlawfully reduced, you should check your employment contract and any company policies related to training and pay. You can also contact the Acas helpline for advice or raise the issue with your employer to clarify how training costs and pay are handled.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any training policies.
Step 2: Confirm if the training is mandatory and how pay is calculated during training.
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice for guidance if you believe deductions are unfair or unlawful.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: My employer says they overpaid me and wants the money back. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says they overpaid you and wants the money back, they can deduct the overpaid amount from your wages, but only if you agree or if your contract allows it. If you don’t agree that you were overpaid, you should explain why and ask your employer to pay you what you believe you are owed. If you were overpaid but cannot afford to repay the full amount immediately, you can ask your employer if they will accept smaller deductions over a longer period.

Your employer must follow certain rules if they are deducting money for overpayments. For example, deductions should not take your pay below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage. Also, if deductions are made for other reasons like damaged property or missing money, there must be a written agreement or contract clause allowing this. If you think your employer has deducted money unlawfully, you should seek advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify the overpayment and discuss repayment options.  
Step 3: If you disagree or cannot resolve the issue, get advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer change my pay without telling me?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer should not change your pay without informing you. Any changes to your pay usually need to be agreed upon by both you and your employer, often through your employment contract or a written agreement. If your employer changes your pay without telling you, this could be a breach of your contract. You should check your contract to see what it says about pay changes and whether your employer has followed the correct procedure.

If your pay has been deducted or changed and you don’t agree with the reason, you should talk to your employer to understand why the change was made. If you cannot resolve the issue by talking to them, you may consider raising a formal grievance. If the problem continues, you might be able to take legal action, such as making a claim for unlawful deduction of wages.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for terms about pay changes.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why your pay was changed.  
Step 3: If necessary, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 4: Seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser if you are a member.  
Step 5: Consider legal options if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What should I do if I have not been paid for work I have already done?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why. It’s best to speak to whoever handles payroll or your manager and explain the problem clearly, showing any evidence like payslips or your contract. If it was a mistake, ask them to pay you straight away rather than waiting until your next pay day.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure. You might also consider seeking help from a Trade Union representative if you have one. If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus a day from when you should have been paid to start this process.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the missing pay.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from a Trade Union or an employment adviser.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal before the deadline if necessary.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I was dismissed without a meeting. Is that unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a sign that your employer did not follow a fair procedure. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer should have a valid reason and follow a fair and reasonable process, which usually includes investigating the situation and giving you a chance to respond, often through a meeting. If your employer dismisses you without a meeting or without following a proper procedure, this could potentially be unfair dismissal.

However, whether your dismissal is unfair depends on various factors, including how long you have worked for your employer and the reason for dismissal. If you have been employed for at least two years, you generally have the right to claim unfair dismissal. You can also appeal the dismissal if you believe it was unfair. It is important to ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement explaining why you were dismissed.  
Step 2: Check how long you have worked for your employer to see if you qualify to claim unfair dismissal.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal with your employer if you believe it was unfair.  
Step 4: If you think your dismissal was unfair and you cannot resolve it with your employer, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me and refused to let me appeal.
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to allow you to appeal, this may be unfair, especially if they have not followed a fair and reasonable procedure. Employers should have a valid reason for dismissal and must act consistently and reasonably. They are expected to follow a fair procedure, which usually includes giving you the chance to appeal the dismissal decision. Denying you the right to appeal could be a breach of this fair process.

You have the right to ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012). This statement must be provided within 14 days of your request. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is often best to try to resolve the issue with your employer first.

Step 1: Request a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Check whether your employer followed a fair procedure, including whether you were given a chance to appeal.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your situation and the possibility of making an unfair dismissal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be dismissed without notice?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, dismissal usually involves your employer ending your employment and giving you notice. However, there are some situations where your employer can dismiss you immediately without notice, such as if you are guilty of gross misconduct like violence. In most other cases, your employer must give you at least the notice period stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer.

Your employer must have a valid reason for dismissal, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure. If you are dismissed, you have the right to be told why, when your employment ends, and your right to appeal. You can also request a written statement of the reasons for dismissal if you have worked for your employer for at least two years (or one year if you started before April 2012). Pregnant employees and those on maternity leave have additional protections.

Step 1: Check your contract for your notice period.
Step 2: Consider the reason your employer has given for dismissal.
Step 3: If you believe you were dismissed without proper notice or unfairly, you can appeal or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What is constructive dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that fundamentally breaches their contract, making it impossible for the employee to continue working there. This could include things like a significant change to job duties, not paying wages, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must leave because of the employer’s conduct, not for other reasons.

To claim constructive dismissal, the employee usually needs to have worked for the employer for at least two years and must resign promptly after the breach. It is important to follow a fair procedure and consider seeking advice before resigning, as this type of dismissal can be complex.

Step 1: Identify if your employer’s behaviour has seriously breached your contract.  
Step 2: Consider whether you have tried to resolve the issue through grievance procedures.  
Step 3: If you decide to resign, do so promptly and keep a record of the reasons.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: How long do I need to work somewhere to claim unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

To claim unfair dismissal in the UK, you generally need to have worked for your employer for at least 2 years. If you have less than 2 years' continuous service, you usually cannot make a claim for unfair dismissal, except in certain cases such as dismissal related to discrimination, whistleblowing, or asserting certain statutory rights. If you started your job before 6 April 2012, the qualifying period was 1 year.

If you believe you have been unfairly dismissed, your employer must have a valid reason for the dismissal, act reasonably in the circumstances, and follow a fair procedure. You also have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have the legal status of an employee and meet the qualifying period.

Step 1: Check how long you have worked for your employer to see if you meet the 2-year qualifying period.  
Step 2: If you qualify, consider whether your employer had a fair reason and followed a fair procedure for dismissal.  
Step 3: If you think your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally or consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer says I was dismissed for poor performance. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says you were dismissed for poor performance, they must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. This means they should have investigated the situation fully and applied a fair and consistent process before dismissing you. Your employer should have given you warnings and opportunities to improve your performance before dismissal, following a fair procedure.

You have the right to be told why you were dismissed, when your employment ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal the decision. If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally and, if necessary, make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have the qualifying period of employment.  
Step 2: Check if your employer followed a fair procedure, including warnings and opportunities to improve.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal with your employer if you think it was unfair.  
Step 4: If unresolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be dismissed while on sick leave?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can be dismissed while on sick leave, but your employer must have a valid reason for the dismissal and must act reasonably in the circumstances. They should follow a fair procedure, including investigating the situation fully before dismissing you. For example, if your employer decides to dismiss you due to long-term sickness absence, they should have explored alternatives and communicated clearly with you. If you are dismissed while on Statutory Maternity Leave, your employer must give you a written statement explaining the reasons for dismissal regardless of how long you have worked for them.

If you believe your dismissal is unfair, you have the right to appeal the decision and your employer must follow a fair and reasonable procedure. You can also ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012).

Step 1: Check if your employer has given you a valid reason and followed a fair procedure.  
Step 2: Request a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have the right to do so.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal if you believe it was unfair.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you need further help.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What should happen in a fair disciplinary process?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A fair disciplinary process in the UK should follow a clear, reasonable, and consistent procedure. The employer must have a valid reason for any disciplinary action and must investigate the situation fully before deciding to dismiss or discipline an employee. The process should be balanced and fair, giving the employee a chance to respond to any allegations against them.

Employers should follow the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures, which includes informing the employee of the issues, holding a disciplinary meeting, allowing the employee to be accompanied if they wish, and giving the employee the right to appeal any decision made. Confidentiality should be maintained throughout the process, and the reasons for any dismissal or disciplinary action should be communicated clearly, preferably in writing.

Step 1: Investigate the issue thoroughly before taking any action.  
Step 2: Inform the employee of the concerns and hold a disciplinary meeting.  
Step 3: Allow the employee to respond and be accompanied if they want.  
Step 4: Make a fair decision based on the evidence and inform the employee in writing.  
Step 5: Provide the employee with the right to appeal the decision.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be made redundant without consultation?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers are generally expected to consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is an important part of the redundancy process and should happen before any final decisions are made. This allows employees to discuss the reasons for redundancy, explore alternatives, and raise any concerns. For individual redundancies, there is no fixed minimum consultation period by law, but it should be meaningful and timely. For larger scale redundancies (20 or more employees), there are specific legal consultation requirements.

If an employer makes someone redundant without any consultation, this could be unfair dismissal, especially if the employee has been continuously employed for two years or more. However, the exact circumstances can vary, so it is advisable to seek further advice if you believe you have been made redundant without proper consultation.

Step 1: Check if you have been given any consultation or notice about the redundancy.
Step 2: If not, raise your concerns with your employer or HR department.
Step 3: If you still believe the redundancy was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What is the difference between redundancy and dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which can happen for various reasons such as misconduct, capability, or redundancy. Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that occurs when an employer needs to reduce their workforce because the job you do is no longer needed. This might be due to business closure, changes in work requirements, or a decrease in work available.

While all redundancies are dismissals, not all dismissals are redundancies. Redundancy involves a fair procedure and valid business reasons related to the job role, whereas other dismissals might be due to employee conduct or performance. Employers must follow a fair and reasonable process for both types of dismissal.

Step 1: Understand the reason for your dismissal—whether it is redundancy or another reason.  
Step 2: Check if your employer has followed a fair procedure and given you proper notice.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me for raising a complaint. Is this unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and you followed a proper procedure. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and act reasonably and consistently in their decision. Dismissing someone for raising a complaint may be seen as victimisation or retaliation, which is generally not allowed under UK employment law.

You have the right to ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012). If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision internally and, if necessary, bring a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important that your employer followed a fair procedure before dismissing you.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal if you think it was unfair.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I challenge my dismissal at a tribunal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

You can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation properly and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining why you were dismissed. If your dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Before making a claim, consider whether you have followed any appeal process your employer offers. Also, be aware that if you sign a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to bring an unfair dismissal claim. Employment tribunal claims usually need to be started within three months of your dismissal. If you want to challenge your dismissal, it is often helpful to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your employment status and length of service.  
Step 2: Request a written statement of reasons for dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 3: Follow your employer’s appeal process if available.  
Step 4: Consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.  
Step 5: If necessary, start an employment tribunal claim within the time limit.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I believe my dismissal was unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, your employer must have had a valid reason for dismissing you and followed a fair and reasonable procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully, acting consistently, and giving you the chance to appeal the decision. You have the right to appeal against your dismissal, and your employer should follow a fair process during this appeal.

You can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012). This statement must be provided within 14 days of your request. If you successfully appeal and get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," and you cannot then make a claim for unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Check if you have been employed long enough to qualify for unfair dismissal protection (usually 2 years).  
Step 2: Request a written statement of reasons for your dismissal if you haven’t already received one.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s appeal process.  
Step 4: If the appeal is unsuccessful and you still believe the dismissal was unfair, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal, but be aware of time limits and seek advice if needed.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: I was treated unfairly at work because of my religion. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. If you believe you have been treated unfairly at work due to your religion, you have the right to raise a complaint with your employer and expect them to take it seriously and investigate the matter.

Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination and harassment related to religion in the workplace. If informal resolution does not work, you can pursue a formal grievance procedure or seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. You may also have the option to bring a claim to an employment tribunal if the issue is not resolved internally.

Step 1: Raise your concerns informally with your employer or HR department.  
Step 2: If unresolved, follow your employer’s formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if necessary.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: My manager is harassing me. Is this discrimination or harassment?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work can be a form of discrimination if it is related to one of the protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, or religion. Harassment means unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates your dignity or creates a hostile environment. If your manager's behaviour fits this description, it could be considered harassment under the law.

If the harassment is not related to a protected characteristic but is still unwanted and makes your workplace uncomfortable, it may be classified as bullying rather than discrimination. Employers have a responsibility to prevent and address both harassment and bullying. You can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and investigate it.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, noting dates, times, and details.
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.
Step 3: If your employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek further advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What is workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. Protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly (treating someone worse because of a protected characteristic) or indirectly (applying a rule that disadvantages people with a protected characteristic).

The law protects employees from discrimination at work, including during hiring, promotion, pay, training, and dismissal. It also covers harassment, victimisation, and bullying related to these protected characteristics. Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination and handle complaints seriously.

Step 1: If you believe you have been discriminated against, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 2: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 3: You can also make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limits set by law.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be treated differently because of my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is unlawful to treat someone less favorably because of a disability. This means you should not be discriminated against at work or in other areas such as education or when using public services due to your disability. Employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments to accommodate disabled employees and ensure they are not put at a disadvantage compared to others.

If you believe you are being treated differently because of your disability, you can raise the issue with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. You may also seek advice or support from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Document any incidents or treatment you believe is discriminatory.  
Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer or through a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options, which may include a discrimination claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What is victimisation at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats you badly because you have made a complaint about discrimination, supported someone else's discrimination claim, or raised a related issue. It is a form of unfair treatment that is prohibited under the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include things like being ignored, given less favourable work, or being bullied because of your involvement in a discrimination complaint.

If you believe you are being victimised at work, you can raise the issue with your employer through informal or formal channels. Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and handle complaints seriously to ensure a safe and fair working environment.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents of victimisation.
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer or HR department.
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: My employer ignored my complaint about discrimination. What can I do?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer ignores your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue again through your employer’s formal grievance procedure, making sure to document your complaint in writing. Employers are legally required to take discrimination complaints seriously and investigate them properly under the Equality Act 2010. If your employer still fails to address the issue, you may consider seeking advice from an external organisation such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

If informal and formal internal steps do not resolve the problem, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Employment tribunals can consider claims of discrimination based on protected characteristics like age, sex, race, disability, and others. It is important to act promptly, as there are strict time limits for making a claim (usually within three months of the discriminatory act).

Step 1: Put your complaint in writing and submit it through your employer’s grievance procedure.  
Step 2: Keep records of all communications and responses from your employer.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on your situation.  
Step 4: If necessary, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Is bullying at work illegal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Bullying at work is not a specific criminal offence in the UK, but it can be unlawful if it amounts to harassment or discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Employers have a legal duty to prevent bullying and harassment and to take complaints seriously. If bullying is related to a protected characteristic such as age, sex, race, disability, or others covered by the Equality Act, it may be considered unlawful discrimination or harassment.

If you experience or witness bullying, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should have policies and procedures to handle bullying and discrimination complaints and take appropriate action to stop it.

Step 1: Check your employer’s bullying and harassment policy.  
Step 2: Report the bullying to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps, including possible legal claims.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What counts as harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, which violates a person's dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. This can include things like offensive jokes, name-calling, bullying, physical attacks, or unwanted physical contact. The behaviour can be repeated or a one-off incident, but it must be related to characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, sexual orientation, or others protected by law.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent harassment and take complaints seriously. If you experience or witness harassment, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should have procedures in place to handle such complaints.

Step 1: Identify if the behaviour is unwanted and linked to a protected characteristic.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not address the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Can my employer refuse reasonable adjustments for my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities to help them overcome disadvantages at work. This duty is part of the Equality Act 2010. Reasonable adjustments might include changes to the workplace, work hours, or equipment used. Employers should not refuse reasonable adjustments without good reason, as failing to make them can be considered discrimination.

If you believe your employer is refusing reasonable adjustments, you can raise the issue informally with them or follow a formal grievance procedure. Employers are expected to take such requests seriously and work with you to find suitable solutions.

Step 1: Inform your employer about your disability and the adjustments you need.  
Step 2: Provide any medical evidence if requested to support your request.  
Step 3: If your employer refuses, ask for a clear explanation and consider raising a formal grievance.  
Step 4: If unresolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your rights and next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: I was treated unfairly because of my race. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, it is illegal to be treated unfairly at work because of your race. Race is a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, which means you are protected from discrimination, harassment, and victimisation related to your race, colour, nationality, or ethnic or national origin. Your employer must not discriminate against you in any aspect of your employment, including hiring, pay, promotion, or dismissal.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly because of your race, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint procedure. Employers are required to take such complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may have the right to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the incidents of unfair treatment, including dates, times, and what was said or done.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, following their grievance procedure if they have one.  
Step 3: If the problem is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a formal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, it is generally unlawful for an employer to dismiss an employee because they have made a complaint about discrimination. Such a dismissal could be considered automatically unfair and may also amount to victimisation under the Equality Act 2010. Employers must have a valid and fair reason for dismissal and follow a reasonable procedure, as outlined by the Acas Code of Practice. If you believe you have been dismissed for making a discrimination complaint, you may have grounds to bring a claim for unfair dismissal or discrimination to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Keep records of your complaint and any related communications with your employer.  
Step 2: Follow your employer’s grievance procedure to raise your concerns formally.  
Step 3: If you are dismissed or treated unfairly after making the complaint, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your rights and possible next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I experience harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you experience harassment at work, your employer has a legal duty to prevent it and take your complaint seriously. You can choose to address the issue informally by speaking to your manager or HR, or formally by making a written complaint following your employer’s grievance procedure. It’s important to keep a record of incidents, including dates, times, what happened, and any witnesses.

Employers should investigate your complaint promptly and take appropriate action to stop the harassment. If your employer does not handle the situation properly, you may be able to take further steps, such as seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice, or ultimately making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Keep a detailed record of the harassment incidents.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or formally.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 4: Consider further legal action if necessary.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
